paper_title: General Multimodal Protein Design Enables DNA-Encoding of Chemistry

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Evolution is an extraordinary engine for enzymatic diversity, yet the chemistry it has explored remains a narrow slice of what DNA can encode. Deep generative models can design new proteins that bind ligands, but none have created enzymes without pre-specifying catalytic residues. We introduce DISCO (DIffusion for Sequence-structure CO-design), a multimodal model that co-designs protein sequence and 3D structure around arbitrary biomolecules, as well as inference-time scaling methods that optimize objectives across both modalities. Conditioned solely on reactive intermediates, DISCO designs diverse heme enzymes with novel active-site geometries. These enzymes catalyze new-to-nature carbene-transfer reactions, including alkene cyclopropanation, spirocyclopropanation, B-H, and C(sp$^3$)-H insertions, with high activities exceeding those of engineered enzymes. Random mutagenesis of a selected design further confirmed that enzyme activity can be improved through directed evolution. By providing a scalable route to evolvable enzymes, DISCO broadens the potential scope of genetically encodable transformations. Code is available at https://github.com/DISCO-design/DISCO.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Enzymes catalyze chemical reactions under mild conditions with high efficiency and specificity, underpinning applications in chemical manufacturing, drug synthesis, and emerging therapeutic modalities. [1, 2] Enzyme engineering has typically relied on iterative rounds of mutation and screening to accumulate beneficial sequence changes toward desired functions an evolutionary process that can enable enzymes to perform functions not previously known in nature. [3] However, every such campaign requires an initial protein with measurable activity. For well-studied transformations, suitable starting points can be found among natural proteins or their close variants; but for many desirable new-to-nature chemistries, nature offers no obvious candidates. Identifying one remains largely matter of chemical intuition laborious process fundamentally limited by what evolution has already sampled. How can we systematically design functional enzymes for chemical transformations that have no precedent in known biology? Generative deep learning models have transformed protein design, successfully enabling the creation of novel binders and the scaffolding of pre-defined structural motifs. [46] However, de novo design of enzymes for new-to-nature reactions remains largely unrealized. Current computational pipelines face two fundamental limitations. First, they rely heavily on pre-specified, fixed geometric arrangements of active-site residues or theozymes. [79] This precludes reactions for which no motif or precise mechanism is known and prevents the discovery of novel active-site geometries. Second, experimentally validated generative pipelines predominantly operate sequentially: they first generate backbone and then devise sequence to match the backbone design (inverse folding). [7, 10, 11] Because sequence and structure jointly determine function, this decoupled paradigm cannot leverage sequence-based objectives during critical stages of backbone formation, and vice versa. We introduce DISCO (DIffusion for Sequence-structure CO-design), general multimodal framework that simultaneously designs protein sequences and 3D structures de novo. DISCO designs can be conditioned on and co-folded with arbitrary biomolecules, without pre-defined residue motifs. novel multimodal Feynman-Kac corrector method allows DISCO to optimize sequence and structure objectives together. DISCO achieves state-of-the-art in silico results in generating binders for diverse biomolecular targets with fine-grained property control. Applied to new-to-nature catalysis experimentally, DISCO generates diverse, evolvable carbene transferases with novel active sites. No inverse-folding is required. These enzymes have activities that surpass typical starting points for directed evolution and, in selected cases, extensively evolved variants. Ultimately, DISCO enables the discovery of biocatalysts and protein motifs previously unknown to biology, expanding the searchable space of DNA-encoded chemical reactivity.

sections.title: Multimodal protein generation with DISCO
sections.content: DISCO models discrete sequences and 3D structures as joint distribution that is denoised through unified generative process  (Fig. 1)  . We achieve this by training single deep neural network that employs masked discrete diffusion process for sequences and, in parallel, leverages continuous diffusion for 3D atomic coordinates. By independently sampling noise per modality during training, one can provably learn the joint reverse process using only unimodal diffusion losses [12]: the standard denoising score matching loss for coordinates and the masked diffusion language model loss for sequences. We trained DISCO using only data from the Protein 2 Figure 1. Multimodal protein design workflow with DISCO. (A) Inference overview, highlighting the arbitrary molecular context, sequence-structure co-generation with sequence correction, and representative applications. (B) Architecture overview. (C) Multimodal inference-time steering schematics with Feynman-Kac corrector. (D) Design-to-test workflow for generating enzymes with new active sites from reactive intermediates. 3 Data Bank without special filtering, avoiding any selection biases introduced when models are trained exclusively on designable structures that folding algorithms can successfully predict. DISCOs architecture comprises an input embedder that produces single and pair representations of all input modalities, frozen protein language model (pLM) that encodes partial sequences, [13] Pairformer stack that contextualizes these representations, and joint diffusion module built on atom-level attention that predicts both sequence logits and denoised coordinates of the backbone (Fig. 1B). [14] We inject SE(3) symmetries softly through data augmentation rather than architectural constraints. Central to our approach is cross-modal recycling mechanism that conditions each generation step on four distinct encodings: the models current predicted clean sequence ˆxseq and 0 structure ˆxstruct . We train structure encoder [15] and employ the pLM to inject sequence information. This bidirectional conditioning ensures that sequence predictions are informed by emerging structural features while structural predictions adapt to evolving sequence identity. , as well as the current noised sequence xseq and structure xstruct τ τ 0 DISCOs inference strategy proved critical to generation quality (Fig. 2B, Fig. S4). Standard masked diffusioniteratively unmasking tokens without revision [16]yields sequences that fail to fold into their generated geometries. By enabling self-correction during sequence inference [17, 18] and introducing novel sequence temperature mechanism that smooths the amino acid distribution for overconfident tokens early in the trajectory, we drastically improve co-designability. Combining this with noisy guidance [12] allows DISCO to achieve state-of-the-art performance in sequence-structure co-design. On unconditional monomer design, approximately 90% of generated sequences refold to within 2 Å RMSD of their designed backbone structures using ESMFold (Fig. 2A, Fig. S5, Table S4). [19] Compared to baselines, DISCO achieves the highest sequence and structure diversity and novelty without sacrificing co-designability (Fig. 2A).

sections.title: Conditioning on arbitrary biomolecular contexts
sections.content: DISCO flexibly conditions on arbitrary biomolecular contexts, including small molecules, metallocofactors, reactive intermediates, and nucleic acids. Non-protein molecules are represented identically to proteins with discrete tokens for chemical identity and corresponding atomic coordinates. The model simultaneously unmasks protein sequences while denoising the coordinates of targets ranging from small molecules and metal cofactors to reactive intermediates and nucleic acids. These contexts co-fold with the designed protein throughout the generative trajectory: DISCO adjusts the coordinates of conditioning biomolecules while designing the protein, capturing conformational changes induced by molecular interactions and extending generation beyond fixed, pre-defined atomic scaffolds. To benchmark this capability, we designed new benchmark, STUDIO-179, library of 179 natural and non-natural ligands spanning applications in catalysis, pharmaceuticals, luminescence, and sensing. The library encompasses rigid molecules (e.g., the pollutant tetrachlorodibenzodioxin), large or flexible molecules (e.g., the cofactor CoQ10), and metals/metalloclusters (e.g., [4Fe-4S]). For each ligand, we quantify the fraction of generated designs that are both structurally diverse and co-designable, defining co-designable as when the protein backbone and all ligand centroids have an RMSD < 2 Å upon refolding with Chai-1. [20] Compared to baselines, DISCO generates the highest proportion of diverse, co-designable complexes for 178 4 Figure 2. DISCO generates diverse, designable protein sequences and structures across wide variety of functions evaluated in silico. (A) In unconditional monomer generation, DISCO outperforms existing methods in terms of co-designability, novelty, and diversity (400 samples). (B) Ablation of unconditional monomer generation with DISCO to identify best inference-time practices. (C) DISCO outperforms existing methods for conditional generation across diverse targets, including natural and non-natural molecules, multi-ligands, DNA, and RNA. (D) Examples of designed (blue) and refolded (white) protein complexes show high agreement. (E) FKC-MM enables the joint steering of sequence and structure, as demonstrated by two multimodal rewards: disulfide bridges and cation-π interactions. Example generated proteins with high disulfide bridge counts are shown at right. (F) FKC-SG generates more proteins with predicted binding specificity (a large separation between binding sites of onand off-targets) compared to existing filtering methods. Example refolded structures are shown at right (on-target in pink, off-target in yellow). 5 of 179 evaluated ligands (Figs. 2C, 2D, Figs. S10, S11, S25). Our formulation naturally supports multi-context conditioningsuch as designing proteins to interface with multiple distinct ligands simultaneouslyagain yielding high-quality complexes (Fig. 2D). Extending beyond small molecules, we evaluated DISCOs performance on macromolecular interfaces. DISCO successfully generated co-designable proteins predicted to bind to both sequence-specific DNA and RNA sequences, outperforming existing models (Fig. 2C). Note that because the frozen pLM in DISCO was not trained on multimeric complexes, we constrained our current evaluation to non-protein-binding tasks. Multimodal inference steering using Feynman-Kac Correctors The multimodality of DISCO enables inference-time scaling with both sequence and structure signals (Fig. 1C). Rather than relying on inefficient generate-and-filter (best-of-N) approaches, which often fail when desired traits are rare, we utilize the Feynman-Kac Corrector (FKC) framework to incorporate target properties into the generation process directly and tilt sample distributions toward having desirable traits without necessarily invoking expensive reward oracles. [21, 22] We derive two novel FKC approaches. First, FKC - Multimodal (FKC-MM) allows for reward tilting with functions defined jointly over both discrete sequences and continuous structures. When using multimodal reward functions for increasing disulfide bond content or cation-π interactions, FKC-MM generates samples with enriched target properties compared to structure-only FKC or unconditional generations (Fig. 2E). Notably, while unconditional generation underrepresents cysteines (Fig. S6), the top 2% of designable, 100-amino acid proteins produced by FKC-MM contain six disulfide bonds density matched by only the top 0.2% of comparable training proteins (Fig. S9). These results underscore the benefit of being able to generate sequences alongside protein structures. We further introduce FKC - Specificity Guidance (FKC-SG) to steer DISCO towards binding exclusively to target molecule while avoiding structurally similar decoy. This is achieved by sampling from tilted distribution that encourages samples that are likely under the on-target model while penalizing samples that are likely under the off-target model (Section A.7). We evaluated FKC-SG on discriminating two dissimilar molecules (e.g., biotin from pyridoxal 5-phosphate (PLP)), as well as very similar molecules, such as aldosterone from cortisone (two constitutional isomeric steroids) and valine from proline (two hydrophobic, aliphatic amino acids). We find that FKC-SG generates proteins that have high separation between onand off-target complexes (Fig. 2F), improving upon filters like best-of-N, which in some cases do not produce any hits passing in silico filters. DISCO designs exhibit realistic protein features with novel, complementary motifs DISCOs designs capture the complex statistical properties of proteins. In unconditional generation, samples display natural amino-acid compositions (Fig. 3A, Fig. S6), diverse secondary structures (Fig. 3B, Fig. S7), favorable local Ramachandran geometries (Fig. 3C), reasonable radii of gyration (Fig. S8), and native-like surface properties including appropriate hydrophobicity and net charge (Fig. 3D). The designs further exhibit high long-range contact order (Fig. 3E), indicating complex, well-connected topologies rather than trivial folds often seen in de novo designs. [23] These properties extend to conditional generation (Figs. S12S17). 6 Figure 3. DISCO designs biophysically realistic proteins with novel motifs, flexible ligands, and target-informed sequences. (A-E) Unconditional generation results: (A) Amino acid type distribution as compared to PDB and inverse-folded samples. (B) Secondary structure diversity. (C) Ramachandran plot. (D) Surface hydrophobicity and net charge. (E) Distribution of long-range contact order. (F-M) Conditional generation results: (F) Folddisco motif similarity searches reveal no close matches to designed residue motifs in AlphaFoldDB. (G) Example active-site (purple) compared to the closest known motif (yellow). (H) Distribution of global sequence/structure novelty. (I) DISCO designs binding sites with consideration of ligand properties, as illustrated by matching lipophilicity. (J) Example design satisfying ligand constraints, building four disulfide bonds to [4Fe-4S] cluster. (K) DISCO-generated conformers are valid as evaluated by PoseBusters, even if they deviate from the reference. (L) DISCO samples different conformers. (M) Examples of sampled conformers that are rare or absent in the training set. 7 We hypothesized that jointly generating sequence and structure allows the designed binding site to adapt its chemistry and geometry as the target adjusts its conformation, yielding mutually compatible interface. This is capability unique to co-design, allowing DISCO to construct chemically appropriate microenvironments without inverse folding or any pre-specified residue motif. Indeed, ligand-conditioned generation yields pockets that are quantitatively responsive to ligand identity: binding-site lipophilicity correlates with ligand hydrophobicity (Fig. 3I, Figs. S20, S21), appropriate coordinating residues emerge for specific cofactors (Fig. 3J, Fig. S27), and sufficiently large cavities form to avoid steric clashes (Fig. S19). Prior co-design models capture these physiochemical trends less consistently (Fig. S22), which aligns with their lower co-designability and diversity. Furthermore, DISCO samples valid ligand conformers beyond the reference input (Figs. 3KM, Figs. S17, S26), by selectively exploring rotatable bonds while preserving rigid geometries. These complementary pockets generated by DISCO exhibit striking novelty and diversity. For coordination spheres up to ten residues, motif similarity searches using Folddisco [24] show that most binding motifs in co-designable generations lack natural homologs in AlphaFoldDB (novel motif defined as no match or RMSD > 3 Å; Figs. 3F, 3G, Fig. S28). Clustering the generated motifs via pairwise distance shows over 90% cluster diversity (Figs. S23, S24). DISCO also generates globally novel sequences, and many folds have no close homologs in the Protein Data Bank (Fig. 3H). Such novelty and diversity are only relevant when the motifs are foldable and physicochemically realistic standards DISCO meet while expanding well beyond the space of known global scaffolds and local motifs. Designing enzymes for new-to-nature biocatalysis Enzyme design is among the most challenging applications for conditional protein generation, especially for new-to-nature catalytic activities that lie outside the models training distribution. Carbene transfer reactions, unexplored in known natural enzymes, have proven valuable for expanding the catalytic repertoire of biology. [25] While directed evolution of cytochrome P450, cytochrome c, and various globin variants has produced numerous carbene transferases, identifying initial activity for desired reaction remains challenging and laborious, usually limited to screening limited set of known scaffolds. Previous computational approaches have designed heme enzymes for only handful of carbene reactions using physics-based theozymes and pre-specified active-site residue geometries [2628]a strategy that is expensive, biased by the choice of motif, and difficult to generalize, especially when the catalytic mechanism is not known. We hypothesized that DISCOs ability to co-generate sequence and structure around arbitrary molecules could bypass residue motif or theozyme specification. Carbene-transfer reactions proceed via formation of an ironcarbenoid intermediate, most often generated by nitrogen extrusion from diazo precursor. This intermediate subsequently transfers the carbene fragment to substrate through diverse pathways, including alkene cyclopropanation, or insertion into CH, BH, SiH, or NH bonds. [25, 29] Formation of the hemecarbene intermediate is generally the rate-determining step (>20 kcal/mol, Fig. S3). [30] Therefore, we conditioned DISCO on DFT-derived geometries and bonding patterns of the hemecarbene precursor complex rather than on complete transition-state model. This choice reflects deliberate simplification: rather than fixing an exact transition state calculated in vacuo, we let DISCOs co-folding mechanism sample conformations of the reactive intermediate that would 8 be compatible with the protein it is constructing. This allows us to bypass the theozyme-based design entirely, where the limited mechanistic understanding for new-to-nature reactions may preclude the design of an accurate theozyme. DISCO is free to explore catalytic solutions without being constrained by human assumptions about the protein residues required for activity or fixed transition state geometry. From 104 in silico generated sequence-structure pairs one to two orders of magnitude fewer than recent structure-based pipelines [7, 10] we applied computational filter (Fig. 1D) combining predicted structures (AlphaFold 3 [14] and Chai-1 [20]), confidence measures such as chain pAE and ipTM, active-site contact counts, solvent exposure, net charge, and exposed hydrophobicity, yielding 90 designs for experimental testing (Section A.10). Neither the sequences nor the structures were redesigned after generation. Computational analysis confirms that the designed active sites represent new residue geometries (Fig. 3F, >80% with >3 Å RMSD in the five nearest residues). Moreover, while some designs closely recapitulate the reference geometry (RMSD < 1 Å), substantial fraction adopt more divergent conformations (RMSD > 2 Å), primarily through rotations about the ironcarbenoid bond (Figs. 3L, 3M). This indicates that DISCO actively explores conformational space even for reaction intermediates. The designed enzymes are highly diverse: no pair shares more than 50% sequence identity, and the 90 designs form 75 distinct structural clusters (TM-score < 0.5). To assess the catalytic generality, we selected four substrates spanning distinct carbene transfer reactions: p-methoxystyrene (1a) with ethyl diazoacetate (EDA, 5a) for cyclopropanation, [31] 1,3-dimethylimidazol-2-ylidene borane (2a) with ethyl 2-diazopropanoate (EDP, 5b) for BH insertion, [32] 1-phenylpyrrolidine (3a) with 5a for C(sp3)H insertion, [33] and tert-butyl-3methyleneazetidine-1-carboxylate (4a) with 5a for spirocyclopropanation of pharmaceutically relevant scaffold. [34] All 90 designs were screened in whole-cell format in Escherichia coli across all four reactions. To control for background reactivity from endogenous hemoproteins, we overexpressed an unrelated protein (TrpB from Thermotoga maritima) under identical conditions. Top hits were selected for validation  (Fig. 4)  . Several reactions exhibited high background activity from endogenous E. coli hemoproteins, which complicated identification of lower-activity designs, especially since overexpressing these designs may disrupt heme homeostasis and further reduce their activity. We note that hemin alone has no detectable activity for the spirocyclopropanation or the C(sp3)H insertion reaction. Remarkably, screening across our selected carbene reactions confirmed that the designed variants were not only functional (2.2 66% exceeded the TmTrpB control) but, in several cases, exhibited high catalytic activity. For the cyclopropanation of p-methoxystyrene, the best design achieved 72% yield and total turnover number (TTN) of 4,050 with 99:1 diastereomeric ratio (Fig. S32), surpassing both early evolved P411 enzymes and the recently reported designed enzyme PNC2 which scaffolded helix bundle around porphyrin-based theozyme (Fig. 4A). [27, 31] For BH insertion, the top variant achieved 98% yield and 5,170 TTN (Fig. 4B), far exceeding both the prior starting point (120 TTN) and the laboratory-evolved variant (2490 TTN). [32] Most notably, for C(sp3)H insertion highly challenging transformation whose previous engineering campaign required 14 rounds of directed evolution and for which mechanistic uncertainty precludes theozyme construction our best design reached 42% yield and 2,360 TTN for the alkylation of 1-phenylpyrrolidine, rivaling the performance of previously evolved P411-CHF catalysts on this substrate (2,030 TTN) (Fig. 4C). [33] The sterically and 9 Figure 4. DISCO-designed carbene transferases (dCTs) catalyze multiple carbene transfer reactions with high activity at room temperature, including (A) p-methoxystyrene cyclopropanation. (B) BH bond insertion of carbenes. (C) C(sp3)H alkylation. (D) Spirocyclopropanation of an unsaturated exocyclic N-heterocycle. Each panel shows reaction scheme, results for hemin/previous evolved variants, plate-based screening data, and yield and TTN results from selected validations. ND: Not Detectable. Figure 5. Novelty of selected, designed enzymes and evaluation of evolvability. (A) dCT-H11 design (green/pink) structure and the AlphaFold 3/Chai predicted structures (grey), alongside the closest PDB match by TM-score. The inset shows the five-residue active-site arrangement predicted by AlphaFold 3 (purple), compared to the closest motif in AlphaFoldDB (yellow). (B) Mutational landscape of error-prone PCR variants in plate-based screening of dCT-H11 for the spirocyclopropanation reaction. (C) Designed and predicted structures of dCT-F9, dCT-G9, dCTH10, alongside the closest PDB matches by TM-score. The five closest active-site residues yielded no full match in AlphaFoldDB. electronically demanding spirocyclopropanation proved more challenging, [34] yielding fewer active variants with modest activity. Although enantioselectivity was not design criterion, modest enantioselectivities were observed across all reactions, with enantiomeric excesses up to 35% for the spirocyclopropanation (Figs. S29S31). Notably, enzymes with preference for either enantiomer were identified for three reactions. To probe the evolvability of DISCOs designs, we selected the spirocyclopropanation reaction, where design alone had yielded only modest activity. We subjected the designed carbene transferase dCT-H11 to single round of error-prone PCR and screened approximately 700 mutants (Fig. 5B). Around 35 variants displayed noticeably improved activity relative to the parent enzyme with divergent enantioselectivity, either enhancing the parents preference (from +35% to +49% ee) or inverting it entirely (+35% to -35% ee, Fig. S33). The top variants harbor substitutions scattered across the protein, consistent with the long-range epistatic effects characteristic of natural enzyme evolution. These results suggest that DISCOs designs can occupy regions of sequence space with accessible uphill paths for further optimization. The top hits span diverse folds with novel motifs  (Fig. 5)  . The closest structural match to 11 dCT-H11 is TetR-family transcription factor from the extremophile Haloarcula marismortui (PDB ID: 3CRJ, TM-score: 0.81) that lacks known catalytic activity. dCT-H11 shares only 21% sequence identity with 3CRJ, suggesting that DISCO repurposed topology not pre-specified for catalysis. The most similar residue motif to the five closest active-site residues, drawn from AlphaFoldDB, shows large mismatch (RMSD > 7 Å), confirming that the geometry of the catalytic residues is itself novel. Other designs are even more divergent: dCT-F9 (closest match: 5MSV, TM-score 0.52, 5% sequence identity) and dCT-G9 (closest match: 7C7E, TMscore 0.51, 9% sequence identity) adopt folds that are substantially more remote from known structures, with no corresponding motif identified in AlphaFoldDB based on their five nearest active-site residues (Fig. 5C). Notably, none of the closest structural matches correspond to naturally heme-binding proteins, highlighting the ability of the model to capture underlying biochemical principles and repurpose unrelated protein folds for heme binding and carbene transfer reactions. Together, these results demonstrate that the model can create novel proteins with emergent functions, extending beyond the boundaries of natural protein space. General biomolecular design unlocks new-to-nature reactivities These results establish general platform for functional protein design in which sequence and 3D structure are generated as coupled object and flexibly conditioned on any biomolecules. Computationally, careful inference alignment across modalities via cross-modal recycling, self-correction, and entropy regularization enables DISCO to generate diverse, high-quality proteins that outperform existing co-generation methods, while inference-time steering on both sequence and structure turns generative sampling into controllable search process. Experimentally, conditioning only on reaction intermediates was sufficient to produce promiscuous carbene transferases across four new-to-nature reactions, bypassing transition-state calculations, theozyme scaffolding, and extensive wet-lab screening. From just 90 genes, top designs achieved 98% yield for BH insertion and >2,300 TTN for C(sp3)H insertion, exceeding previously evolved biocatalysts. Importantly, the designed active-site residues are novel, exhibit stereoselectivity, and occupy accessible fitness landscapes that support subsequent evolution. The ability to genetically encode arbitrary chemical transformations requires global discovery with local optimization. DISCO provides global search for diverse, evolvable enzymes. Yet the reactions explored here represent only small subset of synthetically valuable transformations not found in nature. Mastering increasingly complex mechanisms likely requires closing the loop between generative discovery and directed evolution, informed by richer biophysical constraints. More broadly, co-designing proteins with arbitrary biomolecular partners charts path toward programmable biological systems whose complexity rivals that of natural evolution, and whose function serves human intent.

Lack of Order Control. An analytic Gillespie-style sampler [36] shows that the denoising proceeds by uniformly randomly selecting masked position, offering no control over the generation order of tokens. 2. These two issues have led to variety of solutions involving alternative reverse-time processes for discrete generation. Specifically, we define independent distributions and losses over separate domains as in (S4) and (S8), but replace the modality-specific denoisers with our joint denoiser Dθ, which operates on noisy input containing both modalities: r,t,xpdataxr,tp(xr,tx) 1 σ2 Dstruct θ (xr,t)xstruct2 dαr dr 1 1αr i=1 xi,seq =m δ(xseq)T log Di,seq θ (xr,t) (S17) These tools provide the necessary theory to perform multimodal training and inference. While theoretically any schedule over r, can be used, we lock together the two schedules during inference only by defining the schedules in terms of unifying time variable τ. (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO 24 A.3 Data pipeline We trained on the weighted PDB dataset described in AlphaFold 3 with cutoff date of 202109-30. Data processing, including clustering and filtering, follows the procedures described in Sections 2.5.3 and 2.5.4 of the AlphaFold 3 Supplementary Material. The dataset was obtained by downloading with the Protenix scripts/database/download_training_data.sh script from commit a4059a6894c826b7bb81ba753cbdb89029313244. We sample either single chains or chain-pair interfaces, with interfaces defined as pairs of chains with minimum heavy atom separation less than 5 A. For {chain, interface}, the dataset is weighted as βr Nclust (αprot nprot + αnuc nnuc + αligand nligand) with βchain = 0.5, βinterface = 1, αprot = 3, αnuc = 3, αligand = 1, and Nclust defined by the Protenix dataset clusters. Cropping is performed per sample, where, as in AlphaFold 3 we use contiguous crop, spatial crop, and spatial interface crop. For detailed algorithms, please refer to Section 2.7 of the AlphaFold 3 Supplementary Material [14]. We crop with the following weights: 20% contiguous cropping, 40% spatial cropping, and 40% spatial interface cropping. A.4 Model architecture While our architecture is broadly based on AlphaFold 3, we make number of changes to enable protein sequence and structure co-design. Unless otherwise specified, we make use of the AlphaFold 3 architectural components and the interested reader may refer to the AlphaFold 3 Supplementary Information for detailed module-wise algorithms. As in AlphaFold 3, our model takes the form of conditional diffusion model. However, unlike AlphaFold 3, we remove number of the conditioning modules and add other domain-specific modules. We begin by removing the Template Module from the architecture as well as the MSA Module as computing MSAs for an ever evolving protein sequence trajectory during inference is computationally intractable. To maintain source of evolutionary information, we replace the MSA Module with protein language model, in our case DPLM 650M [13].We present the main model architecture in Algorithm 1. Our tokenization scheme sees each amino acid residue and nucleotide correspond to single token, while for other molecules, we encode each heavy atom as separate token. Input features correspond to those used in AlphaFold 3, with the exception of all MSA and template features (see Table 5 in the AlphaFold 3 S.I. [14]). To obtain the initial trunk features, we feed the input features to the InputFeatureEmbedder (Algorithm 2 in AlphaFold 3 S.I.) We first derive the initial features, {sinputs }, using the InputFeatureEmbedder based on the input features { }. The initial ij Rcz, are generated via linear projections of single and pair representations, sinit the embedded inputs. We enrich the initial pair representations with relative position encodings and token bond features. For cycles beyond the first (c > 1), the architecture employs CrossModalEncode module to update representations based on the current sequence and structure states, xseq , as well as skip connections. The model then integrates evolutionary information via the PlmModule, our drop-in replacement for the MSA Module of AlphaFold 3 and set to be DPLM 650M [13], updating the pair representations using features from the underlying protein language model. To encode chain breaks as input for the pLM, we insert length 25 glycine linker between all protein chains. To obtain the single representations, we average the hidden states for each transformer block, while for the pair representations, we concatenate all attention weights for each attention block. Following an update to the single representation si, both representations are processed through the PairformerStack. As we believe that for de novo design we require lighter pairformer than for typical folding tasks, we reduce the number of blocks in the Pairformer from 48 in AlphaFold 3 to 8. A.4.5 Atom attention decoder The AtomAttentionDecoder (Algorithm 5) maps the processed token-level features back to the required output space. Token activations ai are broadcast to their constituent atoms and summed with the corresponding atom-level skip connections qskip . This representation is passed through 3-block, 4-head AtomTransformer using cross-attention. If the decoder is operating in sequence mode (seq_mode = True), the updated atomic features are aggregated back into token-level representations using scatter-add operation. Smooth LDDT Loss. The Smooth LDDT Loss measures the accuracy of predicted atomic coordinates relative to ground truth structures. Specifically, the masked diffusion reverse process requires that once particular token is unmasked, it retains its value throughout the rest of the inference trajectory. This, however, prevents the model from correcting its mistakes and, for optimal performance, requires the model to have learned perfectly how to generate particular sequence in every n! possible unmasking orderings. Given limited compute budgets learning, such model is impractical, and significant performance gains have been observed by allowing masked discrete diffusion model to self-correct its errors [17, 18, 40]. We similarly find that using standard masked discrete diffusion inference performs poorly. To remedy, this we adopt random remasking strategy [17] wherein at each sequence time we uniformly choose percent of tokens to be masked and 1 percent to be unmasked with the full procedure summarized in Algorithm 8. This allows the model to correct mistakes it made during the inference trajectory, especially at critical structural residues such as those of loopy regions of structure and at regions where the structure shifts from disordered to structured. A.6.5 Conditional generation Conditional generation of proteins in complex with other biomolecules extends naturally from our framework. During inference, the conditioning molecule (e.g. small-molecule ligand or nucleic acid chain) is provided as an input feature: its reference conformer geometry and bond information are supplied through the standard AlphaFold 3 conditioning features, and its atomic coordinates are allowed to be denoised throughout the reverse diffusion trajectory. The protein sequence and structure are generated jointly in the same setting as above, while the conditioning molecules features participate in attention and pairformer computations, allowing the model to shape the protein around the target binding partner (and vice versa). No additional training or fine-tuning is required. Noise Scheduler Schedule t1.5 A.7 Inference steering Inference-time steering enables the adaptation of single pretrained model to diverse and evolving design objectives at sampling time, without the need for retraining. Desired properties are incorporated directly into the generation process, tilting the distribution of samples at each step. Common guidance approaches rely on heuristic score modifications in diffusion models, which only approximately target conditional distributions and often compromise control over the resulting marginals. In this work, we instead consider the Feynman-Kac Corrector (FKC) framework [21], which enables principled sampling from desired target distributions. In the continuous case, this is done by updating samples using weighted stochastic differential equation (SDE). The Feynman-Kac PDE describes the time-evolution of the density of samples as (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO (x) pFK = , pFK (x)vt(x) + g2 2 pFK (x) + pFK (x)(ht(x) (cid:90) ht(x)pFK (x)dx) , (S34) where the first two terms correspond to the Fokker-Planck PDE and the last term is reweighting PDE, assigning each sample time-dependent log-weight wt based on the weighting function ht(x). The first pair is amino acids valine (on-target) and proline (off-target); the second is steroids aldosterone (on-target) and cortisone (off-target); the third is biotin (on-target) and pyridoxal 5-phosphate (PLP, off-target). For each, we sweep over inverse temperatures β {0.5, 1.0, 2.0}. Previous work also found that resampling at every step reduced sample diversity significantly due to variance of the weights[21], and so we also searched for the best time point to stop resampling (resample = false when τ {0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1.0}). Finally, we tried particle batch sizes of {2, 4, 8}. We found that β = 0.5 worked best, as well as setting resample = false when τ = 0.6. For the valine/proline pair, we used 4 particles and for the other two pairs, we found 8 particles were better. A.7.3 FKC-SG evaluation For each ligand pair, we generated 200 protein structures and sequences of length 150 using DISCO with FKC-SG or DISCO on its own. We refolded all 200 sequences with the on-target using Chai-1 and aligned them with the structure generated by DISCO. If the complex was designable (i.e. RMSDs of both the ligand centroids and proteins were less than 2 Å), we kept the structure. We then folded each off-target with the sequence and computed the RMSD between the ligand centroids after aligning the complex with the protein folded with the on-target. For each batch of particles, we kept the sample with the largest ligand centroid RMSD (max reward) for further evaluation (max. 200 samples for each method if each batch had one designable complex). For each protein sequence, we folded it three times with Chai-1 for each of the on/off-targets. For each sequence, we measured the pairwise ligand centroid RMSD between all remaining Chai-1 folds (9 comparisons) and kept the minimum RMSD. sequence was considered to show separation between the onand offtargets if this minimum RMSD exceeded fixed threshold (6 Å). We report the number of such specific sequences for each condition. A.8.4 Conditional evaluation of small-molecule ligands We assembled curated set of 179 chemically diverse molecules (cofactors, metals, druglike compounds, fluorophores, saccharides, pollutants, metabolites, etc.), including multiple different molecules. We call this benchmark STUDIO-179. For each molecule, we optimized the geometry of RDKit ETKDG conformer with the semiempirical method GFN2-xTB[47]. The RDKit-derived bond information and the optimized geometry were used as the input bond/reference conformer information to the model. 50 proteinligand complexes were generated at each of three target lengths (150, 200, and 250 residues) using DISCO with the inference settings specified in Table S3. Co-designability was assessed by refolding each complex with Chai-1 [20]. design was considered co-designable if both: RMSDbackbone(Xdesign, XChai) < 2.0 Å, design ℓ ℓ Chai < 2.0 Å ligand ℓ, (S52) (S53) where ℓ denotes the centroid of ligand ℓ. For multi-ligand systems, Eq. (S53) was required to hold for every ligand independently. proteinligand steric clash was flagged if any protein heavy atom in the backbone structure 0.5 Å, where rvdW denotes the van der + rvdW and ligand heavy atom satisfied dij < rvdW Waals radius and the 0.5 Å tolerance accounts for minor overlap in experimental structures. For logP and pocket hydrophobicity analysis, the binding site is defined as the set of protein residues whose backbone atoms lie within 10 Å of any heavy atom of the cofactor/ligand, retaining up to the 10 closest residues sorted by minimum proteinligand distance. A.8.5 Conditional evaluation: nucleic acid binding We selected DNA/RNA sequences that were released in the PDB after our training cut-off date. Each DNA chain was cropped to maximum length of 10. Designed proteinnucleic acid complexes were refolded with Chai-1. We followed the experimental design of the RFDiffusion3 DNA benchmark and for all DNA and RNA targets generated 100 samples with lengths sampled uniformly between lengths 50 to 80. Co-designability was assessed by first aligning nucleotide reference atoms between the design and the Chai-1 prediction, using phosphorus (P) atoms as the primary anchor. design was considered co-designable if RMSD (global-aligned) backbone (Xdesign, XChai) < 2.0 Å, (S54) (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO 50 ensuring that the protein refolds correctly and maintains the designed spatial relationship with its nucleic acid partner. A.8.6 Baseline methods For unconditional monomer generation, we benchmarked against La-Proteina[48], ProteinGenerator[49], ESM3[50], RFdiffusion3[10], and BoltzGen[11]. For conditional generation (small-molecule and nucleic acid binding), only RFdiffusion3 and BoltzGen were included, as the remaining baselines do not natively support ligandor nucleic acidconditioned generation. We additionally included RFDiffusion All-Atom [51] as baseline for small molecule conditioning. For each designed backbone for RFDiffusion All-Atom, one sequence was generated using LigandMPNN[15], and was selected for downstream evaluation. All baselines were run with publicly released code and recommended default parameters; the same downstream evaluation pipeline was applied identically. For RFDiffusion3, BoltzGen, and RFDiffusion All-Atom to allow for comparison to DISCO we require each to co-fold the ligand or nucleic acid positions along with the protein backbone. A.8.7 Binding motif novelty To assess whether DISCO generates novel binding motifs, we searched designed active sites against known structures using Folddisco[24] with default parameters. For each co-designable proteinligand complex, the binding motif was defined as the protein residues closest to the ligand (by side chainligand heavy-atom distance of refolded structure with Chai-1). Searches were performed on Chai-1 refolded structures (which provide side-chain coordinates required by Folddisco) against the afdb_swissprot_v4 Foldcomp database. Folddisco calculations are only carried out for co-designable structures, to avoid searching for ligands whose binding site cannot be agreed upon by Chai-1 and DISCO. binding motif was classified as novel if either: (i) Folddisco returned no match, or (ii) Folddisco returned full match to all query residues but the matched-residue RMSD exceeded 3.0 Å. A.8.8 Binding motif diversity We now turn to assess the diversity of the generated binding motifs. The binding motifs definition is the same as Section A.8.7. Given two K-residue motifs (P, τP) and (Q, τQ) from co-designable proteins sharing the same ligand type, we report the aggregated per-sample metric of nearest-neighbour diversity NNi = minj=i d(i, j) for metric detailed below. Furthermore, we also report (i) Cα-RMSD. Both coordinate sets are centred and then iteratively aligned using the Kabsch algorithm [52] with the Hungarian (linear-sum-assignment) optimal matching. At each of = 3 iterations, the current assignment is solved on the squared-distance cost matrix, and the optimal rotation is recomputed on the matched pairs. The final RMSD is dRMSD = (cid:113) k=1 pk qσ(k)2, where σ is the terminal assignment. 1 (ii) Chem-cost. combined spatialchemical distance is used. Each residue is assigned chemical class (hydrophobic, polar, positive, negative, or special). chemical distance matrix Dchem is defined as 0 for identical residue types, 0.5 for same class, and 1.0 otherwise. The ij (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO 51 Hungarian assignment is solved on Cij = α pi qj2 + β Dchem (α = 1, β = 5), alternated with Kabsch alignment for = 3 iterations. The reported score is the mean per-residue combined distance under the final matching: dchem = 1 (cid:0)α pk qσ(k) + β Dchem (cid:1). ij k,σ(k) ij = pi pj and DQ (iii) 1 lDDT. After the spatial (RMSD) assignment σ is established, the internal distance matrices DP ij = qσ(i) qσ(j) are compared. For every upper-triangular pair (i, j), the absolute difference DP ij is tested against thresholds {0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0} Å. The local distance difference test score is lDDT = (preserved pairs)/(pairs thresholds), and we report 1 lDDT so that larger values indicate greater diversity. ij DQ (iv) Frobenius distance. This rotation-invariant metric compares the internal Cα distance matrices directly. An optimal residue permutation is found by iteratively solving Hungarian assignment on the row-permuted distance-matrix cost (n = 3 iterations). The Frobenius distance (cid:114) is dF = i<j(DP ij DQ π(i),π(j))2. 1 (K 2) (v) Motif cluster diversity. With all pairwise Cα-RMSD values between motifs using iterative Hungarian matching with Kabsch alignment, we perform complete-linkage agglomerative clustering with distance threshold of = 2.0 Å. The cluster ratio is defined as = nclusters/ndesigns, where 1 indicates that nearly every design adopts structurally distinct active-site configuration, while 0 indicates that designs collapse onto small number of shared motif geometries. A.9 Density Functional Theory calculations Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed with ORCA 6.0.1 using the hybrid B3LYP functional in combination with the def2-TZVP basis set [53]. Dispersion effects were included with Grimmes D3 correction and BeckeJohnson damping (D3BJ).Unless otherwise noted, all species were treated as triplet-state open-shell systems and were therefore computed using unrestricted DFT. Solvent effects were modeled with the SMD implicit solvation model using ethyl acetate as the solvent, chosen to approximate the dielectric environment of the enzyme active site. For carbene insertions, we optimized the pre-reactive intermediate corresponding to the approach of the carbene precursor to the heme iron center. With heme, multiple carbene insertion intermediates were computed. The heme cofactor was modeled with 5-methylimidazole axial ligand; this ligand was included in the electronic-structure model but removed in the subsequent design stage. All reaction intermediates were geometry optimized and characterized by vibrational frequency analysis. For the calculation of energy barriers, transition state geometries were located using the NEB-TS method and frequency analysis indicates that they possess single imaginary frequency along the reaction coordinate. A.10 Design filtering for enzymes For every enzyme design campaign, DISCO generated on the order of 104 sequencestructure pairs conditioned on the target intermediate structure (including reference conformer and bonds), where DISCO generates the structure for the reactive intermediate. These candidates were subjected to multi-stage filtering pipeline combining orthogonal structure-prediction oracles, physicochemical filters, and diversity-aware selection. Each candidate was first refolded with Chai-1, and structures satisfying the co-designability (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO 52 Figure S3. DFT-calculated reaction mechanism for the carbene-insertion reactions using Feporphyrin as the cofactor. All design campaigns used intermediate 1 reoptimized with heme b. criterion (Eq. (S53)) were advanced to second round of structure prediction with AlphaFold 3. In both rounds, the protein was co-folded with the reactive intermediate to assess activesite geometry. AlphaFold 3 predictions were additionally performed with only the cofactor (omitting the intermediate). design was retained only if it was co-designable under both Chai-1 and AlphaFold 3. Dual-oracle passing designs were subjected to the following filters: 1. Contacts. The number of protein heavy atoms within 4.0 Å of any intermediate heavy atom was required to be 5. 2. Active-site burial. Three complementary metrics assessed ligand burial. First, the SASA burial fraction, fburial = 1 Acomplex Afree , (S55) was required to exceed 0.5. Second, we computed solid-angle enclosure score. From each of up to 15 ligand heavy-atom origins (selected by farthest-point sampling), = 500 rays were cast uniformly over S2 via Fibonacci lattice. ray from origin was considered blocked by protein atom if cos (ˆr, ˆdj) cos arctan (cid:32) rvdW + rprobe pj (cid:33) , (S56) (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO 53 where ˆr is the ray direction, ˆdj the unit vector from to protein atom pj, rvdW its van der Waals radius, and rprobe = 1.4 Å. The enclosure from each origin is the fraction of blocked rays; the worst-case (minimum) across all origins was required to exceed 0.5. Third, the maximum exposure angle was estimated from the centroid origin by clustering unblocked rays via greedy single-linkage and reporting the bounding-cone half-angle of the largest cluster; this was required to be 65. 3. Metal coordination. For metalloenzyme campaigns, proper coordination geometry of catalytic metal ions was verified (correct number and identity of coordinating residues, bond lengths within 0.5 Å of ideal values). 4. Surface hydrophobicity. The fraction of solvent-exposed hydrophobic residues (Ala, Val, Ile, Leu, Met, Phe, Trp, Pro) was required to be <50% to reduce aggregation propensity. 5. Net charge. The formal net charge at pH 7 was checked to be within 15. Designs were then ranked within each cluster by simple composite confidence score: = 0.5 ipTM + 0.25 pTM 0.5 PAEchain + 1.0 ipTMchain, (S57) where ipTM and pTM are the AlphaFold 3 global interface and predicted TM-scores, PAEchain is the pairwise aligned error between the protein and ligand chains, and ipTMchain is the pairwise interface pTM between the ligand and protein chains. In addition to the composite score, hard cutoffs were enforced: ipTM 0.7, pTM 0.7, and chain-pair ipTM 0.5. Passing designs were clustered at both the sequence level (MMseqs2, 30% identity) and the structure level (Foldseek, TM-score 0.5). Within each sequence cluster, at most 2 sequences were retained. Within each structure cluster, at most 8 structures were retained. (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO 54 A.11 Additional in-silico results A.11.1 Additional unconditional generation results We note that as co-designability is very high for unconditional generation results, sample analysis of generated proteins (e.g. amino acid distribution, secondary structure distribution, radius of gyration, relative contact order, etc.) show no qualitative changes when filtered for co-designability. A.11.2 Additional conditional generation results We note that while co-designability is not as high as unconditional generation results, sample analyses of generated proteins generally showed no qualitative changes when filtered for co-designability. The only significant change can be seen in Fig. S13. With the exception of motif novelty and diversity (and unless otherwise noted), all sample analysis are evaluated without co-designability filtering. Like unconditional generation analysis, general properties (e.g. conformer PoseBusters validity, steric clashes with ligands, logP of ligand vs pocket chemical features) reflect whether the model captures reasonable chemical fidelity; once this is satisfied and the protein is co-designable, only then does motifs novelty and diversity matter (e.g. one can easily sample random pockets that do not fold). sections.title: B In vitro methods and results
sections.content: B.1 Materials and instrumentation All chemicals were obtained from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. Racemic product standards 1b, 2b, 4b were prepared according to literature-known methods.[34, 54, 55] M9-N buffer was used for enzymatic reactions and was prepared from 5X stock solution of Na2HPO47H2O (64 g), KH2PO4 (15 g), and NaCl (2.5 g) dissolved in 1 of double-distilled water (ddH2O). The working solution (1X) was prepared by dilution with ddH2O, followed by addition of CaCl2 (1 mL, 0.1 M) and MgSO4 (2 mL, 1.0 M). The pH was adjusted to 8.0. The TAE buffer used in DNA gel electrophoresis was prepared from 50X stock solution made by dissolving Tris free base (242 g), disodium EDTA (18.61 g), and glacial acetic acid (57.2 mL) in 1 of ddH2O. The 1X TAE buffer was then prepared by diluting 40 mL of the 50X stock solution with 1960 mL of ddH2O. PCR amplifications were performed using the Phusion High-Fidelity PCR Kit (New England Biolabs) on an Eppendorf Mastercycler X50s. Gibson Assembly reactions were carried out using an in-house solution containing isothermal master mix, T5 exonuclease, Phusion DNA polymerase, and Taq DNA ligase (New England Biolabs).[56] Gene fragments (without adapters) were ordered from Twist Bioscience, where the vendor was chosen for speed, cost and quality reasons. T7 Express competent Escherichia coli (BL21) (New England Biolabs) were used for all transformations. All NMR spectra were obtained at the Caltech Liquid NMR Facility. NMR spectra were collected on Bruker Prodigy 400 MHz instrument equipped with cryoprobe operating at 400 MHz and 101 MHz for 1H and 13C, respectively. 1H spectra are referred to residual CDCl3 solvent signals referenced at δ 7.26 ppm. Data for 1H NMR are reported as follows: chemical shift (δ ppm), integration, multiplicity (s = singlet, = doublet, = triplet, = quartet, = pentad, sext = sextet, hept = heptet, = multiplet, br = broad singlet), and coupling constant (Hz). High-performance liquid-chromatography mass spectroscopy (HPLCMS) for standard reaction screening analysis was performed using an Agilent 1200 series instrument equipped with an Agilent Poroshell C18 column (Agilent Poroshell 120 EC-C18, 4.6 50 mm, 2.7 µm) with Poroshell 120 guard column (4.6 5 mm, 2.7 µm). Water and acetonitrile (MeCN) with 0.1% (v/v) HPLC grade glacial acetic acid were used as eluents. Normal phase chiral HPLCUV data was collected with an Agilent 1260 Infinity HPLC equipped with Daivel Chiralpak IC column (4.6 250 mm, 5 µm) and Daivel Chiralpak IB column (4.6 250 mm, 5 µm) run in series (for products 2b and 3b) or Daicel Chiralcel OD-H (product 1b). Hexanes and isopropanol were used as eluents. Gas chromatography (GC) analyses were conducted on an Agilent Technologies 7820A GC system equipped with flame ionization detectors (FID). An Agilent J&W HP-5 column was used for achiral analyses, while an Agilent J&W CycloSil-B column was employed for chiral (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO 73 separations. GCMS analyses were carried out on an Agilent 7820A GC coupled to an Agilent 5977B MSD using an Agilent J&W HP-5MS UI column. Chromatographic purification was accomplished by flash chromatography using Biotage Isolera One instrument with Sfär High-Capacity Duo columns using AMD Silica Gel 60, 230400 mesh. Ethyl acetate in hexanes was used as eluents. Centrifugation was carried out in an Allegra 25R tabletop centrifuge equipped with TS-5.1-500 swinging bucket rotor or S5700 microplate swing bucket rotor. B.2 Cloning and transformation in 96-well plates The pET22b(+) backbone (Novagen) was PCR-linearized, digested with DpnI, gel-purified and confirmed to produce minimal background transformation. All genes were inserted between the NdeI and XhoI restriction sites. Ordered gene fragments were delivered in lyophilized form (Twist Bioscience) and resuspended in PCR-grade water to concentration of 50 ng/µL. We note that for design sequences that did not begin with methionine, an ATG codon was added as start codon. His-tag with the amino acid sequence LEHHHHHH was appended to the end of each sequence. Assembly reactions were performed in 96-well format using Gibson Assembly. Each reaction contained 1 µL insert DNA, 1 µL vector (100 ng/µL), and 5 µL Gibson Assembly mix, followed by incubation at 50 for 1 h. Transformation was performed by incubating 5 µL BL21(DE3) competent cells with the assembly mixture on ice for 30 min, followed by heat shock at 42 for 10 s. After recovery in LB medium (100 µL), 10 µL was used to inoculate 500 µL LB medium supplemented with 100 µg/mL carbenicillin (LBcarb), and cultures were grown overnight at 37 C. Transformants were verified by in-house sequencing (LevSeq), showing 95% correct sequences.[57] The remaining 5% likely resulted from sequencing, assembly, or transformation errors. Variants were organized in 96-well plates, including: negative control: β-subunit of tryptophan synthase from Thermotoga maritima (TmTrpB, wells A1, B2, C3, D4) and positive control: evolved protoglobin from Aeropyrum pernix 5312[34] (wells E5 and F6). Glycerol stocks were prepared for long-term storage. B.3 Protein expression and reaction screening in plates Glycerol stocks were used to inoculate 500 µL LBcarb in 96-well plates, covered with sterile, breathable film and grown at 37 with 220 rpm shaking. From stationary-phase cultures, 50 µL was transferred to 900 µL TBcarb and incubated for 2 before induction with 50 µL of isopropyl β-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG) (0.5 mM final) and 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) (1 mM final) in TBcarb. Cells were harvested by centrifugation (4,000g, 10 min) and resuspended in 380 µL M9-N buffer (pH 8.0) supplemented with 10 g/L glucose by orbital shaking at 900 rpm. The plates were then transferred into Coy anaerobic chamber ( 0 70 ppm O2) to set up the reactions anaerobically. Reactions were initiated by adding 20 µL of substrate solution in MeCN ([substrate]final = 10 mM, [EDA]final = 15 mM, final MeCN concentration: 5%). Plates were sealed with adhesive aluminum foil and incubated overnight at room temperature with shaking (700 rpm). Workup procedures (reaction dependent): For 1b, 3b and 4b: The plates were removed from the Coy chamber, and HPLC-grade MeCN (1200 µL) was added to each well. The resulting suspensions were mixed by shaking on an (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO 74 orbital shaker at 500 rpm for 20 min at room temperature, and the plates were then centrifuged (5,000g, 15 min, 4 C) to remove protein and cell debris from the supernatant. The supernatant (200 µL/well) was transferred to polypropylene 96-well microtiter plate (Agilent) and sealed with an Easy Pierce Heat Sealing Foil (Thermo Scientific) and subjected to reversed-phase HPLCMS analysis using methods details in Table S5, Table S6, and Table S7. The product MS peak was integrated using the built-in analysis. Table S5. LC-MS methods for analysis of compound 1b. Solvent A: H2O + 0.1% AcOH, solvent B: MeCN + 0.1% AcOH. Flow rate: 1.5 mL/min. Time (min) (%) (%) 50.0 50.0 50.0 50. 0.0 2.5 Table S6. LC-MS methods for analysis of compound 3b. Solvent A: H2O + 0.1% AcOH, solvent B: MeCN + 0.1% AcOH. Flow rate: 1.5 mL/min. Time (min) (%) (%) 15.0 15.0 90.0 95.0 95.0 15.0 15.0 85.0 85.0 10.0 5.0 5.0 85.0 85.0 0.0 0.4 0.5 2.1 2.5 2.6 3.0 Table S7. LC-MS methods for analysis of compound 4b. Solvent A: H2O + 0.1% AcOH, solvent B: MeCN + 0.1% AcOH. Flow rate: 1 mL/min. Time (min) (%) (%) 50.0 50.0 50.0 50.0 0.0 3.0 For 2b: The plates were removed from the Coy chamber, and 400 µL of 1 mM solution of 1,2,3-trimethoxybenzene dissolved in 1:1 (v/v) EtOAc:cyclohexane were added to each well. Plates were sealed using 96-well sealing mat and then mixed by shaking on an orbital shaker (900 rpm) for 20 min. Plates were then centrifuged (5,000g, 10 min, room temperature), and the organic layer (200 µL/well) was transferred to 400 µL glass insert within 2.0 mL screw-cap vial for GCMS analysis. Analyses were performed on an Agilent J&W HP-5MS UI column using the following oven program: 120 (1 min), ramped at 35 C/min to 250 C, and then ramped at 75 C/min to 300 (hold 0.2 min) for total run time of approximately 11.2 min. The internal standard and product peaks were integrated using built-in MassHunter Quantitative Analysis software (Agilent). B.4 Protein expression and whole-cell validation reactions Cultures expressing enzyme variants with promising activity were streaked from the corresponding glycerol stocks onto LBcarb agar plates. single colony was used to inoculate 5 mL of LBcarb and grown overnight at 37 with shaking at 250 rpm. Subsequently, 0.5 mL of the starter (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO 75 culture were used to inoculate 50 mL of TBcarb in 125 mL sterile Erlenmeyer flask. In parallel, plasmid DNA was isolated from the starter culture using MonarchPlasmid Miniprep Kit (New England Biolabs), and the sequence of each variant was confirmed by Sanger sequencing (Quintara Biosciences, Culver City, CA). Cultures were grown at 37 (220 rpm) to an OD600 of 0.60.8, cooled on ice for 30 min, and induced with IPTG (0.5 mM final) and ALA (1.0 mM final). Expression proceeded at 22 for 2024 h. Cells were harvested (4,000g, 5 min), and pellets were resuspended in M9-N buffer (10 g/L glucose) and normalized to OD600 = 30. Aliquots (380 µL) were transferred to microcentrifuge tubes and placed in Coy anaerobic chamber. Reactions were initiated by adding 20 µL substrate solution ([substrate]final = 10 mM, [EDA]final = 15 mM, final MeCN concentration: 5%). Reactions were incubated overnight at room temperature (700 rpm), and each variant was tested in triplicate. B.4.1 Analysis by GC-FID (compounds 1b - 4b) Tubes were removed from the Coy chamber, and 400 µL of 1:1 (v/v) EtOAc:cyclohexane containing an internal standard (see section Calibration curves for GC-FID analysis) were added to each tube. Tubes were vortexed and then centrifuged (20,000g, 10 min, room temperature), and the organic layer (200 µL) was transferred to 400 µL glass insert within 2.0 mL screw-cap vial for GCFID analysis. Analyses were performed on an Agilent J&W HP-5 column using the following oven program: 100 (2 min), ramped at 50 C/min to 300 (hold 2 min) for total run time of 10 min. The internal standard and product peaks were integrated using built-in analysis software (Agilent) and quantified using calibration curves. B.4.2 Analysis by LCMS (compound 4b for directed evolution) Tubes were removed from the Coy chamber, and 1.2 mL of MeCN was added to each tube. After mixing, the tubes were centrifuged (20,000xg, 15 minutes, 4 C), 300 µL of the supernatant and 50 µL of stock solution of papaverine hydrochloride (internal standard, 6.5 mg in 450 mL 2:1 MeCN:H2O) were transferred into glass inserts within 2.0 mL screw-cap vial for reversedphase HPLC-MS analysis (method details are in table S8.). The internal standard (papaverine hydrochloride) and product peaks were integrated using built-in analysis software (Agilent) and quantified using calibration curves prepared and measured at the same time as sample analysis (see section Calibration curves for LC-MS analysis). Table S8. LC-MS methods for analysis of compound 4b (for directed evolution). Solvent A: H2O + 0.1% AcOH, solvent B: MeCN + 0.1% AcOH. Flow rate: 1 mL/min. (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO 79 B.7 Directed evolution Random mutations were introduced using error-prone PCR (epPCR). PCRs were run using Taq polymerase (New England Biolabs) in the presence of 300, 400, and 500 µM MnCl2 with the primers shown in table S9 following reported procedure.[58] Primers 005 and 006 were used to amplify the DNA region encoding the full-length protein of interest. Primers 007 and 008 were used to amplify the backbone (pET22b(+) vector) fragment containing gene encoding for ampicillin resistance using Phusion polymerase and the template plasmid DNA. PCR products were isolated, and fragments were then assembled into circular plasmid using the Gibson assembly protocol, incubating at 50 for 1 hour. Then, 2.5 µL of the Gibson product were used to transform 10 µL of competent E. coli cells. Transformed cells were supplemented with 150 µL SOC medium and immediately plated on LBcarb agar plates. Plates were incubated at 30 for 1618 hours. Single colonies were then used to inoculate 500 µL LBcarb 96-well plates; eight wells on each plate were inoculated with single colonies of the parent enzyme (parent controls), and two wells were inoculated with sterile toothpick (sterile controls). Expression, reaction setup and work-up in 96-well plates was done as described in section B.3. total of eight plates were screened by reversed-phase HPLCMS, and sequences were verified by in-house sequencing (LevSeq).[57] The highest-performing variants were validated in triplicate as described in section B.3, and the enantioselectivity was measured (see figure S33). interest and internal standard (1,2-diphenylethane) were dissolved in 1:1 (v:v) solution of cyclohexane and EtOAc to create two separate 100 mM solutions. Seven different standard-to-internal standard (S/IS) ratios were prepared: 1:1, 0.5:1, 0.25:1, 0.125:1, and 0.0625:1 by mixing specific volumes of the 100 mM solutions in GC vial. The total volume for each sample was adjusted to 500 µL by adding cyclohexane:EtOAc (1:1, v/v). An aliquot of the prepared solutions was transferred to 400 µL glass insert within 2.0 mL screw-cap vial and subjected to GC-FID analysis with an achiral stationary phase. For 2b: The analyte of interest was prepared under conditions analogous to those used for enzymatic reactions. 200 mM stock solution in MeCN was prepared, from which successive stocks of 100, 20, 10, 2 and 1 mM were generated. From each stock, 20 µL were added to 380 µL of reaction buffer (M9-N, pH 8.0, 10 g/L glucose), followed by 400 µL of 1:1 (v/v) cyclohexane:EtOAc solution containing the internal standard (1,2,3-trimethoxystyrene, 1 mM). Tubes were vortexed and centrifuged (20,000g, 10 min, room temperature), and 200 µL of the organic layer were transferred to 400 µL glass insert in 2.0 mL screw-cap vial for GCFID analysis. The IS and peaks were integrated using built-in analysis software (Agilent). Calibration curves were generated by plotting the known concentrations of the product, based on prepared S/IS ratios, as function of the ratio of its FID signal (peak area) relative to that of the internal standard. The slope of the calibration curve was used to calculate the product yield in enzymatic reactions. (Back to Table of Contents) DISCO B.9.1 Calibration curves for 1b Figure S34.
